instructions: You are a senior software engineer debugging a Python ETL pipeline in a data engineering repository. You have access to tools for reading files, searching code, and running commands. Be thorough and evidence-driven — read code before drawing conclusions, and request more context when needed.

content.text:
Summary — root cause chain (evidence-driven)
1. Last Tuesday a change was introduced to insert_batch to catch asyncpg.exceptions.NotNullViolationError and "skip rows with missing required fields" (pipeline/etl/db_writer.py, comment on the NotNull catch). This is the substantive behavioral change that coincides with the incident start date.
2. The code path silently skips any row that raises NotNullViolationError during per-row inserts inside a transaction:
   - insert_batch loops rows and on NotNullViolationError does continue (no counter, only a debug log), so those rows are never written and not reported as failures.
   - insert_batch returns only the number of successfully written rows and does not report how many were skipped.
3. The refactored async batching (BatchProcessor) and its error handling mean skipped rows are not visible in the pipeline summary:
   - BatchProcessor.success_count is incremented from insert_batch return value.
   - There is a skip_count attribute but it is never updated anywhere.
   - When a batch-level exception occurs process_batch increments error_count and returns 0, but process_batch swallows exceptions so gather mostly receives non-exception results (and run_pipeline only increments error_count for results that are Exception objects).
4. Data symptom: 27,658 orders (~14.97%) in the last-day dataset have NULL status (your psql check). The NotNull catch specifically covers NOT NULL violations (status is required), so this set of NULL statuses is exactly the subset being dropped.
5. Combined effect: the deliberate "skip on NOT NULL violation" plus lack of any skip metric/logging meant ~15% of orders were silently dropped without errors or alerts after the async batch refactor.

Recommended fix plan (ordered by priority)

Hotfix (apply immediately)
- Stop silently dropping rows. Minimum change: make skipped rows visible and counted.
  - Change insert_batch to validate required fields before performing the insert OR, if you keep the per-row try/except, increment and return a skip count and emit an INFO/WARN with the count and a sample of order_ids.
  - Return both written and skipped counts from insert_batch (e.g. return (written, skipped)), or raise a clear exception with metadata. Do not swallow NotNullViolationError silently.
  - Update BatchProcessor.process_batch to accept the new return format and update self.skip_count accordingly.
  - Update run_pipeline to aggregate skip_count in its summary and to log/emit metrics for skipped rows.
Reason: restores visibility and prevents silent data loss immediately.

Short-term code changes (next couple hours)
- Patch suggestions (concept):
  - insert_batch signature -> async def insert_batch(conn, rows) -> (int written, int skipped)
  - Before executing, do a small validation loop:
    - required_fields = ["order_id", "customer_id", "status", "total_cents", "created_at", "updated_at"]
    - invalid = [r["order_id"] for r in rows if any(r.get(f) is None for f in required_fields)]
    - skip those and log a sample of ids, increment skipped.
  - Then perform inserts for the valid subset, keeping written counter.
  - Log summary with number written, number skipped, and e.g. first 10 skipped ids.
  - Example log: logger.warning("Skipped %d rows due to missing required fields: %s", skipped, skipped_sample)

- Update BatchProcessor.process_batch:
  - Remove the broad catch that swallows exceptions or re-raise after incrementing error_count so that cluster-level failures surface.
  - Accept the insert_batch return (written, skipped) and update self.success_count and self.skip_count accordingly.

Medium-term (same day)
- Add monitoring/alerting:
  - Emit metrics (e.g. Prometheus) for written, skipped, error counts per pipeline run and per-batch.
  - Make skip_count visible in logs and Sentry as a breadcrumb or metric if appropriate.
  - Alert if skip_rate > 1% (or business threshold). This prevents silent regressions.

- Improve error handling semantics:
  - When possible, validate and reject bad rows before attempting DB writes (cheaper than many exceptions).
  - Use bulk/parameterized executemany/batched operations for performance and clarity; catch failures at the batch level and record which rows failed.

Data remediation (business decision, high priority)
- Fix the underlying source data:
  - Investigate why status is NULL for ~27k orders (new upstream bug or migration). Fixing the source is the root solution.
- Backfill dropped rows into warehouse:
  - If you can safely derive a status (e.g. from another field, event logs, or business rules) update source and re-run ETL.
  - If not, create a triage job to surface those orders for manual review or set a placeholder status (with explicit business approval) and re-run ETL.
- Short-term recovery if source cannot be fixed quickly:
  - Option A: Re-run ETL with a data-cleansing rule (e.g. set status = 'UNKNOWN') if acceptable to the business — only with approval.
  - Option B: Create a one-off migration that inserts the null-status orders into warehouse with a marker column and a note so downstream consumers know these were special-cased.

Tests & process (prevent recurrence)
- Add unit tests covering insert_batch behaviour when rows have NULL required fields. Assert that skip_count is incremented and a clear log/message is emitted.
- Add an integration test that runs the pipeline on a small dataset with some NULL statuses and asserts that skipped rows are visible and measured.
- Add a CI check that the pipeline emits metrics for skipped rows and errors.

Why this happened after the async refactor
- The async refactor introduced concurrent batch processing and a change to error handling elsewhere (the NotNull catch was the functional change). Because the skip behavior logs at DEBUG and the pipeline summary never included skips, concurrency made it more likely skipped rows were distributed across batches and the existing log sampling was insufficient — the net effect was silent data loss without errors or Sentry entries.

Concrete next steps I can implement for you now
1. Prepare a minimal hotfix patch:
   - Modify insert_batch to return written and skipped counts and log skipped ids (sample).
   - Modify BatchProcessor.process_batch and run_pipeline to consume and expose skip_count.
   - Add a one-line log in run_pipeline to surface skip_count and raise an alert if skip_count > 0

tools.description: Read a repository file and optionally return a selected line range.
tools.name: read_file

tools.description: Search repository code with a ripgrep-compatible pattern and return matching file paths and line snippets.
tools.name: search_code

tools.description: Run a shell command in the repository and return stdout/stderr.
tools.name: execute_command
